item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm confused as to why Agent Teller had preferences for other brands of beer prior to having his first beer. Is this a statement on American commercialism?

Also, the last memories this girl has are of being burned alive. Surely that should play into this somehow?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Mental age is an actual, quantifiable, concept in psychology. Basically how ones intelligence score results compare to an average person of a particular chronological age. The issue isn't with mental age, but with chronological age.

Presumably what we are taking about with 4469-A is her chronological age as assessed from her physiologic age. In other words, how old her body appears to be. They could get s measure of tht parameter from her telomere length.

Telomeres being repetitive sequences found at the ends of our chromosomes. They are how organisms with linear DNA genomes avoid the problem of 5' ends (also called the end replication problem).

+ WTF is the end replication problem?
- hide this content

I was going to describe it, but there are lots of good explanations out there so I will just include a few some
https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/pmc/articles/PMC2819049/
https://courses.lumenlearning.com/boundless-biology/chapter/dna-replication/
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ohm-avnDYcM.


Bottom line is that telemeres get shorter as we age to the tune of about 25-28 base pairs a year and they could calculate a body chronological age from this data.

I do have to agree that the month and year is far more specific than is possible or reasonable given the error inherent in these assessments, of both mental age and chronological age.

Borrowing from SimpleCadences's suggestion, I would rephrase is this way:
""UPDATE: SCP-4469-A has exhibited accelerated emotional development and mental maturation since containment. At the present rate of psychological development, her mental age is predicted to align with to her body's chronological age (estimated from telomere length) in the early 2030's.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
One nitpick:

An empty bottle of Heineken lager beer. Agent Teller claims to have a strong distaste towards all Heineken products but recalled a period where he drunk his first beer during an acquaintance's party. Agent Teller does not remember why his first alcoholic beverage was Heineken despite having preferences for other brands at that time.

Why is the Foundation confused here? If that was his first beer then he couldn’t have known he doesn’t like Heineken at the time.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"Dr. Collins is later warned against the active usage of SCP-4469 and has been forbidden from testing SCP-4469's properties by research staff until granted permission."
I believe the term should be "was" not "is" in this case.
Otherwise, an excellent article. I was initially under the impression that this would be a "thing does a thing" but after close consideration, it deemed itself much more worthy. Nice allusion to burial there, as well. +1

item.title: Admin Post - Info - Open
item.content:encoded: Oskeo, instead of replying to your own comments, please edit your previous post using the "edit" function under the "options" tab to the lower right of every comment. That prevents spam buildup, and it's in the rules.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Also, +1, of course

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Really well done. I was enraptured. I'd like to see more testing logs if you revisit it.

What happens if a researcher writes another researcher's name on it? Will it hold the ink or will it come off similarly to how the shovel avoids blemishes from wear? I feel that should be logged.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
At the very least, I wonder if you might rethink this statement:

"UPDATE: SCP-4469-A has begun to increase in intellect and intelligence. Subject's mental age is theorized to reach her normal age by late September of 2033."

It's not particularly clinical in tone, for example what is a "normal age" in this context? Also, how could the Foundation possible quantify exactly what someone's "mental age" is, much less with such precision that they can predict an exact month at which it would presumably "catch up" to her biological age?

I think you can just drop that line completely. If you really want to keep it for some reason, perhaps change it to something like "UPDATE: SCP-4469-A has exhibited accelerated emotional development and mental maturation since containment, more consistent with her apparent biological age than a teenage individual. Observations are ongoing."

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting premise, but you didn't take it in an especially interesting direction.

Frankly, I disagree with this. It's interesting forbme purely because of the implications that strong attachments to people can also affect empathic objects such as this.

It's just a strange circumstance, which can make for great fiction if you can leave the reader feeling curious, bemused, or unsettled.

Which is meant to imply that seemingly benign and normal objects like this can literally ressurect the dead. It was never meant to be some big story; just some flash in the pan article with a twist.

But whatever, your opinion, my opinion.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Interesting premise, but you didn't take it in an especially interesting direction.

A lot of articles, I feel, overuse children and animals as easy targets to evoke sympathy. You almost had me at the twist when Collins dug up his daughter, alive and well and aged appropriately, but it doesn't really go anywhere from there. It's just a strange circumstance, which can make for great fiction if you can leave the reader feeling curious, bemused, or unsettled. This didn't leave me feeling anything. Novote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This could use a solid once-over for grammatical and punctuation errors, I'm seeing a lot of uncapitalized words and missing punctuation. You could also stand to tighten up the clinical tone a bit more, but it's significantly less of an issue. Otherwise, I like the story you're telling here and the idea of digging up your dead daughter's older, living body is plenty strange.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I'm not necessarily saying you should add that experiment in, and in fact I agree that removing the whole exchange is probably easier and makes more sense. The root issue I have is that it comes across as way too convenient that the subject remarks about her random doll without prompting and the researcher happens to have it on hand. The possible experiment is just fridge logic that pulls me even further out of it once I think about it more.

This was just an example of how the dialogue didn't ring true to me. But I don't want to keep dogging you about it or anything, if you are still happy with the way it reads then you do you!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Or happening to mention the exact stuffed animal that the interviewer brought with them, which also means the Foundation is wasting a chance to test whether she can recognize her stuffed animal from an assortment of false ones, to test whether she actually has the memories she claims to have. I could go on.

For the record, that would take way too long to set up and really doesn't serve much in the service of the article. Sure, it'd make sense to use the scientific method to prove it, but when has any of the articles on the wiki ever done that in general?

Like, sure it would fill in some continuity holes, but for the sake of convenience and general tonal consistency of the article out-of-universe (because remember: this is still a story wrapped in a document), nothing much can be done that's already been accomplished.

Well, you win some, you lose some.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Just to restate what I said before, the dialogue is very artificial to my ear. Stuff like using past tense to refer to her own life, one that she (as far as I can tell) believes she is still living. Or happening to mention the exact stuffed animal that the interviewer brought with them, which also means the Foundation is wasting a chance to test whether she can recognize her stuffed animal from an assortment of false ones, to test whether she actually has the memories she claims to have. I could go on.

That is a subjective call of course, so if other readers don't agree then you are welcome to ignore me!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Her dialogue doesn't fit because it's stilted and expositing key bits of information that the author wants to get across to the reader, or clearly setting up the next response from the interviewer.

Sorry if it came off that way but, no, it's stilted that way because this is a kid in the body of a grown woman that crawled out of the ground. She's going to have jarring dialogue because of experiencing something like this. Not to mention she has gaps in her memory between the accident that killed her, and the events that lead up to it.

As for the "expository" parts, I did not at all plan for setting anything up. In fact, I tested it out by saying out loud what I was putting down there for the dialogue to make sure that it didn't sound plain.

So to me, it feels forced and artificial in the sense that I can clearly see the voice of the author, which isn't something that one can handwave away as being an in-universe mystery. It doesn't really matter who is saying it, because it reads to me as being artificial from the get-go.

Except this dialogue is written in the account of me having to say out-loud first to test if it sounds normal. I needed to think first what kind of words would a young person would say in a situation like this.

I get that this is how it felt to you personally, but i want to be crystal-clear that this was not the intention. Dialogue is difficult to write for, so having to say what to put down in an interview log helps a lot.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Her dialogue doesn't fit because it's stilted and expositing key bits of information that the author wants to get across to the reader, or clearly setting up the next response from the interviewer. So to me, it feels forced and artificial in the sense that I can clearly see the voice of the author, which isn't something that one can handwave away as being an in-universe mystery. It doesn't really matter who is saying it, because it reads to me as being artificial from the get-go.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Is it really her, though? Because it might not makes sense for real her to say, but if it's not her…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I think the interview log could use some reworking to make it more believable. As written it really doesn't sound like a 14 year old to me, or a real human being at all. Like, ask anyone to share anything they can remember about their life, and I just can't imagine them starting with "I used to live in a two-story house. It was a good neighborhood". That sounds more like a dispassionate, third party account of someone's life than the first thing someone trying to remember their own life would say. Likewise, it strains credulity that one of the very first things the subject would recall is a specific stuffed animal that she had, which also had her name conveniently written on it, a detail that she would feel compelled to mention immediately and unbidden, which hey the interviewer just so happened to have behind his back.

Stuff like that makes it pretty obvious that the dialogue was written with exposition in mind rather than as plausible things a person would say, and that just tears me out of the article.

There are a couple other places where the article's tone is a little uneven, like the postscript about the subject's "mental age", which is not a thing that can really be quantified to the precision implied by the note, or a few of the notes in the experiment log.

I think you can fix those things up though, so I'm a novote for now.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Well, this went a completely different direction than I expected. And was pretty heart wrenching… either way +1 from me

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Good eye, fix'd

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Well done. One concern, the replacement of the signatures on the shovel is unclear. The way it is written, the last signature placed on the shovel is replaced when one is added. This would create a never changing list of 11 people who could use the shovel and one user who was replaceable.

Did you actually mean for the *oldest* signature to be replaced? That would create a cycling list of the last 12 people to sign.

Regardless, +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this. It started off simple but interesting enough that even without the resurrection of Sarah I would have up voted based on the test logs alone.

I do have a few (possibly nitpicky) concerns. I find it a little bit hard to understand exactly why the foundation kept Michael from using it. Sure, there may have been personal motivation, but there's no implication of him going against regulations in any way. If he was doing so in a controlled environment, I don't see why the foundation would have a problem. Other SCPs are used for personal gain of employees at times, and unless there was a clear danger at play (of which there is none here) the foundation is usually a-okay with it. If you are to add or rewrite part of this I would either remove their restrictions on him or write some reason in why the foundation would care.

Also, I don't quite understand that last blurb. Are they giving amnestics to Sarah or her parents? The blurb seems to imply that it's meant to stop her parents from forming attachments, which makes little sense if they were given to her.

Regardless, I enjoyed this article. +1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Idk about this one. On one hand, I like what you did with Dr. Collins and that was gut-punching. On the other hand, I wanted to see his reaction to what happened, not the daughter's. Like to make him know that even though he got what he wanted, he won't be able to see her and she won't be able to remember him. That would have made a bigger reaction than just an interview of her describing a weird event. No-vote for now, still thinking whether to upvote or not.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Dang. +1

item.title: Author Post
item.content:encoded:
coldpost? coldpost.

yeah, I got nothing to add here. I should've posted this sooner but school got in the way and I was feeling lazy :P

Source of picture: here.
